questions.question_text: Ohio is what type of foreclosure state?
questions.explanation: Ohio is strictly a judicial foreclosure state. All foreclosures must go through the court system — there is no power-of-sale or non-judicial foreclosure option available.

questions.question_text: In an Ohio foreclosure, how is the property ultimately sold?
distractors: By the lender directly to a buyer
questions.explanation: Ohio judicial foreclosures end with a sheriff's sale after the court issues a judgment. The property is sold at public auction conducted by the county sheriff.

questions.question_text: In Ohio, until what point can a borrower redeem their property during foreclosure?
questions.correct_answer: Before the court confirms the sale
distractors: Within 6 months after the sheriff's sale
Up to 1 year after the judgment
At any time before the deed is recorded
questions.explanation: Ohio's right of redemption allows the borrower to redeem the property before the court confirms (approves) the sheriff's sale. Once confirmed, the right expires.

questions.question_text: A home sells at an Ohio sheriff's sale for $150,000, but the mortgage balance is $200,000. Can the lender pursue the borrower for the $50,000 difference?
questions.correct_answer: Yes, deficiency judgments are permitted in Ohio
distractors: Only if the borrower agrees in writing
questions.explanation: Ohio permits deficiency judgments. If the foreclosure sale price is less than the outstanding debt, the lender can sue the borrower for the difference.

questions.question_text: What is the typical timeline for an Ohio judicial foreclosure from filing to sale?
questions.explanation: Ohio judicial foreclosures typically take 6-12 months from the initial filing to the sheriff's sale. The process involves filing a lawsuit, service, hearings, judgment, and sale scheduling.

questions.question_text: Ohio is a lien theory state. This means that when a borrower takes out a mortgage:
questions.correct_answer: The borrower retains title and the mortgage creates a lien
distractors: The lender holds title until the loan is paid
Title is held by a neutral third party
The state holds title until recording
questions.explanation: In lien theory states like Ohio, the borrower retains title to the property. The mortgage creates a lien (security interest) on the property but does not transfer title to the lender.

questions.question_text: Ohio's recording system is described as 'race-notice.' What does this mean for competing claims?
questions.correct_answer: First good-faith recorder without notice of prior claims wins
distractors: The first person to record always wins regardless
The person with the oldest deed always takes priority
Recording order does not affect the priority of claims
questions.explanation: Under race-notice, a subsequent purchaser who records first AND had no notice of a prior unrecorded claim will prevail. Both recording priority and good faith/lack of notice matter.

questions.question_text: Which Ohio deed provides the greatest protection to the buyer?
questions.explanation: A general warranty deed provides the broadest protection, warranting title against all defects — including those arising before the grantor's ownership. It is the strongest form of deed.

questions.question_text: A grantor wants to transfer property without making any warranties about the title. Which deed should they use?
questions.explanation: A quitclaim deed transfers whatever interest the grantor has without any warranties. The grantee has no recourse against the grantor if title problems are discovered later.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases property but does not record the deed. A subsequent buyer purchases the same property in good faith and records first. Under Ohio's race-notice system, who prevails?
questions.correct_answer: The subsequent buyer who recorded first in good faith
distractors: The first buyer since they purchased first
Neither, until a court determines priority
The seller, who retains title by default
questions.explanation: Under Ohio's race-notice system, a subsequent purchaser who records first and had no notice of the prior unrecorded deed prevails. This underscores the importance of prompt recording.

questions.question_text: Under an Ohio land installment contract, who holds legal title during the payment period?
questions.explanation: In a land installment contract (ORC Chapter 5313), the seller retains legal title while the buyer makes installment payments. Title transfers only when the contract is fully paid.

questions.question_text: A buyer on a land installment contract has made payments for 6 years and paid 25% of the purchase price. The buyer defaults. What termination method must the seller use?
questions.explanation: When the buyer has paid for 5+ years OR paid 20%+ of the purchase price, the seller must use judicial foreclosure to terminate the contract. Both thresholds are met here.

questions.question_text: A buyer on a land contract has paid for 2 years and has paid 10% of the purchase price. They default on payments. What process can the seller use?
questions.explanation: When the buyer has paid for less than 5 years AND less than 20% of the purchase price, the seller may use the faster forfeiture process with a 30-day notice rather than judicial foreclosure.

questions.question_text: An Ohio land installment contract must be recorded with which office?
questions.explanation: Ohio requires land installment contracts to be recorded with the county recorder. Recording provides constructive notice of the buyer's equitable interest in the property.

questions.question_text: A buyer on a land installment contract has paid for 4 years and 22% of the purchase price. They default. What termination process must the seller use?
questions.correct_answer: Judicial foreclosure, since 20% has been paid
distractors: Either forfeiture or foreclosure at seller's choice
questions.explanation: The buyer has paid 22% of the purchase price, exceeding the 20% threshold. Even though less than 5 years have passed, either threshold alone triggers the judicial foreclosure requirement.
